title: Caching strategies: write-through, aside, back
markdown:
## Why this matters

Caching is the highest-leverage performance tool in system design and the most common source of subtle correctness bugs. "How do you keep the cache consistent with the database?" is a question with no perfect answer — only trade-offs you must be able to articulate.

## Core mechanics

### Cache-aside (lazy loading)
The application owns the logic: read → miss → load from DB → populate cache. Writes go to the DB and **invalidate** (delete, not update) the cache key. This is the default pattern: simple, cache failures are non-fatal, and only requested data is cached. Costs: every miss pays a DB round trip plus a write to the cache, and there is a race — two concurrent readers on a miss both hit the DB; worse, a reader can populate stale data if it read the DB before a concurrent write committed.

### Write-through
Writes go to the cache, which synchronously writes to the DB. Reads are always warm for recently-written data, and cache and DB never diverge for written keys. Costs: write latency includes both systems, and you cache data that may never be read.

### Write-back (write-behind)
Writes go to the cache and are flushed to the DB asynchronously, in batches. Spectacular write throughput; used inside databases themselves (page caches, LSM memtables). Cost: a crash before flush loses acknowledged writes — only acceptable with replication of the cache layer or for data you can afford to lose.

### Invalidation: delete, don't update
On write, deleting the cache key beats updating it: updates race with each other (two writes can land in cache out of order) while deletes converge — the next read repopulates from the source of truth. Pair with TTLs as a backstop for missed invalidations.

### Stampede protection
When a hot key expires, thousands of requests miss simultaneously and crush the DB (dogpile). Defenses: per-key request coalescing/locking (one loader, others wait), probabilistic early refresh (refresh before expiry with probability rising as TTL nears), or serving stale while revalidating in the background.

## Trade-offs and failure modes

- Cache-aside risks brief staleness between DB commit and invalidation; write-through pays latency for consistency; write-back trades durability for throughput.
- TTLs are a blunt safety net: short TTL = more DB load, long TTL = longer staleness windows.
- Negative caching (caching "not found") prevents repeated misses for absent keys but must be short-TTL or creation events appear delayed.
- Hot-key failure: if one key takes 100k req/s and its cache node dies, the DB inherits that load instantly. Replicate hot keys or layer an in-process cache above the distributed one.

## Real-world example

Facebook's memcache deployment (the NSDI '13 paper) is the canonical cache-aside-at-scale story: deletes on write, leases to prevent both stampedes and a stale-set race (a lease token must accompany the set after a miss), and regional invalidation streams driven by the DB replication log so caches in every region converge.

## Interview framing

Name cache-aside as your default, then immediately address invalidation (delete on write + TTL backstop) and stampede protection — interviewers probe those two. If asked about strong consistency, say so plainly: caches give you eventual consistency; if a read must be linearizable, read the DB.

## Key takeaways

- Cache-aside with delete-on-write and TTL backstop is the production default.
- Update-in-place on write races; deletion converges.
- Write-back buys throughput by risking acknowledged-write loss.
- Every hot-key design needs a stampede story: coalescing, early refresh, or stale-while-revalidate.
key_takeaways: Cache-aside with delete-on-write invalidation plus a TTL backstop is the default production pattern.
Delete instead of update on writes: concurrent updates race, deletes converge.
Write-through buys read-after-write warmth at write latency cost; write-back buys throughput at durability cost.
Hot keys need stampede protection: request coalescing, probabilistic early refresh, or stale-while-revalidate.
Caches are eventually consistent by nature; linearizable reads must go to the source of truth.

questions.prompt: On a write in a cache-aside system, why is deleting the cache key generally safer than updating it with the new value?
choices: Deletes are cheaper than sets in most caches
Concurrent updates can land in the cache out of order leaving stale data forever; deletes force the next read to repopulate from the source of truth
Updating the cache requires a distributed transaction
Deleted keys are automatically repopulated by the cache itself
questions.correct_answer: Concurrent updates can land in the cache out of order leaving stale data forever; deletes force the next read to repopulate from the source of truth
questions.explanation: Two racing writers can set the cache in the opposite order to their DB commits, pinning stale data until TTL. Deletion converges because the next reader reloads from the DB.

questions.prompt: Which write strategy can lose acknowledged writes on a crash?
questions.explanation: Write-back acknowledges the write once it is in the cache and flushes to the database later; a crash in between loses the write unless the cache layer itself is replicated/durable.

questions.prompt: A hot cache key with a 5-minute TTL expires and your database immediately receives 40,000 identical queries. Name this failure mode and describe two distinct mitigations.
questions.correct_answer: This is a cache stampede (dogpile). Mitigations: (1) request coalescing or per-key locking so only one request reloads while others wait or get stale data; (2) probabilistic early refresh or background refresh so the key is renewed before expiry; (3) stale-while-revalidate, serving the expired value while one background fetch updates it.
questions.explanation: Any two of coalescing/locking, early refresh, or stale-while-revalidate demonstrate understanding; the key insight is collapsing N concurrent loads into one.
